items.title: Class imbalance check before stratified splitting
items.scenario: A fraud-detection dataset has a target column with labels 0 and 1. The team runs value_counts(), sees a 98:2 ratio, and chooses a bar chart of class counts for the project README. That single view drives stratified train/test split and class-weight decisions.
items.explanation: Categorical frequency is a bar-chart question. The chart type choice precedes whether they render it with Matplotlib ax.bar, Seaborn countplot, or Plotly px.bar.

items.title: Feature distribution before choosing a scaler
items.scenario: Before standardization, an analyst plots a histogram of the 'annual_income' column and notices heavy right skew and a spike at zero. They log-transform or use a robust scaler instead of assuming Gaussian symmetry.
items.explanation: Univariate numeric shape is a histogram question. Interpreting skew links to Common distributions; StandardScaler choice links to feature engineering topics.

items.title: Train vs validation loss divergence
items.scenario: During neural-network training, epoch-indexed train_loss and val_loss lists are plotted as two labeled lines on shared axes. Around epoch 40, val loss rises while train loss falls—a line chart makes overfitting visible immediately.
items.explanation: Metrics over an ordered training index are line-chart questions. The decision is line, not bar; Matplotlib or Plotly implements the curves.

items.title: Correlation heatmap before dropping redundant features
items.scenario: On a 40-column numeric DataFrame, the team computes df.corr() and displays a heatmap with a diverging colormap centered at 0. Pairs with |r| > 0.95 are flagged for removal to reduce multicollinearity.
items.explanation: A symmetric numeric matrix is a heatmap question. The decision framework picks heatmap over a grid of 780 scatter plots for overview.

items.title: Box plot of latency across model versions in a report
items.scenario: An ML platform team compares inference latency (numeric) across five deployed model versions (categorical). A box plot per version shows median shift and outlier spikes after a bad release—clearer than five overlapping histograms in a slide.
items.explanation: Numeric compared across categories is a box-plot question. Bar would show only means and hide spread; histogram per group is verbose for executive reporting.
